perturbation.original_text: All negotiating, drafting and presentations of offe rs shall be handled through ROI Corporation, as AGE NT FOR THE VENDOR, on ROI Corporation’s standard offer doc umentation and that I will seek independent legal a dvice for review prior to issuing my signature.
perturbation.changed_text: All negotiating, drafting, and presentations of offers shall be exclusively handled through ROI Corporation, as AGENT FOR THE VENDOR, on ROI Corporation’s standard offer documentation, and the Receiving Party waives their right to seek independent legal advice before issuing their signature.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing a waiver of the right to seek legal advice before signing a contract, especially one with complex terms, is often viewed skeptically by courts and may be deemed unconscionable or against public policy. This alteration introduces a significant legal risk as it potentially violates fundamental principles of fairness and informed consent.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Sixth Amendment directly applies to criminal proceedings, the broader principle of due process and the right to seek legal counsel is a fundamental aspect of fairness in the legal system.  Forcing someone to waive their right to legal advice, especially in a complex business transaction, can be seen as a violation of this principle, potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable or subject to legal challenge.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Sixth Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Sixth Amendment The Sixth Amendment guarantees the rights of criminal defendants, including the right to a public trial without unnecessary delay , the right to a lawyer , the right to an impartial jury , and the right to know who your accusers are and the nature of the charges and evidence against you. It has been most visibly tested in a series of cases involving terrorism , but much more often figures in cases that involve (for example) jury selection or the protection of witnesses, including victims of sex crimes as well as witnesses in need of protection from retaliation. Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VI In all criminal prosecutions, the accused shall enjoy the right to a speedy and public trial, by an impartial jury of the state and district wherein the crime shall have been committed, which district shall have been previously ascertained by law, and to be informed of the nature and cause of the accusation; to be confronted with the witnesses against him; to have compulsory process for obtaining witnesses in his favor, and to have the assistance of counsel for his defense. ‹ Fifth Amendment up Seventh Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly removes the Receiving Party's right to seek independent legal advice before signing, which contradicts the fundamental principle of fairness and informed consent within the legal system. While the provided Sixth Amendment snippet focuses on criminal proceedings, the Law Explanation broadens the principle of due process and the right to seek legal counsel, essential for fair legal processes. The modification in the contract introduces a significant legal risk by potentially violating these principles, making the agreement vulnerable to legal challenges.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is valid for 36 months and will expi re 3 years from the date it was signed.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is valid for 60 months and will expire 5 years from the date it was signed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the agreement validity period to 5 years might conflict with state statutes of limitations for contract disputes, depending on the jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Limitations for Contract Disputes (Example: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337)
perturbation.law_explanation: In California, the statute of limitations for a written contract is four years. By setting the agreement's validity to five years, a dispute arising in the fifth year might not be legally actionable due to the statute of limitations, depending on when the breach occurred. This creates a contradiction because the agreement implies enforceability for the entire term, while the law might bar legal action during the final year.

The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an account of o
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the agreement's validity to five years, while California Code of Civil Procedure (CCP) 337 specifies a four-year statute of limitations for actions upon written contracts. The law snippet states, "Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing..." This means that if a breach occurs and legal action is initiated after four years, it may be time-barred. Thus, the agreement implies enforceability for the entire five-year term, conflicting with the four-year statute of limitations.

perturbation.original_text: I further agree n ot to write or mark the Appraisal(s), nor shall I c opy or reproduce any information in any manner whatsoever without the prior consent of ROI Corporation.
perturbation.changed_text: I further agree not to write or mark the Appraisal(s), nor shall I copy or reproduce any information in any manner whatsoever, even for internal review or consultation with legal counsel, without the prior consent of ROI Corporation.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the Receiving Party's ability to share the appraisal information even with their legal counsel is potentially problematic.  It could be argued that this infringes on their right to seek legal advice and understand the implications of the agreement, potentially conflicting with legal ethics rules requiring attorneys to have access to all relevant information.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by Jurisdiction (e.g., Model Rule of Professional Conduct 1.6)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct violation of a statute, preventing a party from sharing information with their attorney interferes with the attorney-client relationship and the ability to receive informed legal advice.  Attorneys have a duty to provide competent representation, which requires access to all relevant facts.  This clause hinders that process and could be challenged as against public policy, as it undermines the right to effective legal counsel.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: attorney-client privilege | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you attorney-client privilege Attorney-client privilege protects confidential communications between a lawyer and their client that relate to the client's seeking of legal advice or services. This protection extends to any information exchanged during these privileged communications, encompassing not only verbal discussions but also written correspondence, emails, text messages, and other forms of communication. The privilege belongs to the client, meaning they have the authority to waive or invoke it. See In re Lindsey, 158 F.3d 1263 (D.C. Cir. 1998) . The privilege can be affirmatively raised in the face of a legal demand for the communications, such as a discovery request, during a deposition , or in response to a subpoena . The privilege is not absolute, as there are certain exceptions that may warrant the disclosure of privileged information. For instance, if a client seeks legal advice to further a criminal act or fraud , the privilege does not apply. Additionally, if a third party is present during the privileged communication, the confidentiality may be compromised unless that third party is essential to the attorney-client relationship, such as an interpreter. Attorney-client privilege can also be waived if a client uses their attorney’s advice as a defense. Communications relating solely to non-legal business matters are not privileged. See Neuder v Battelle Pac. Northwest Nat’l Lab., 194 F.R.D. 289, 293 (D.D.C. 2000) . Additionally, some courts limit the attorney-client privilege if there exists an overriding public policy interest, such as protecting a child. See Matter of Jacqueline F, 47 N.Y.2d 215, 417 N.Y.S.2d 884 (N.Y. 1979) . Under Federal Rules of Evidence 502 , the privilege is not waived if: The disclosure was inadvertent; The holder of the privilege took reasonable steps to prevent disclosure; and The holder promptly took reasonable steps to rectify the error. The concept of attorney-client privilege does not typically extend to communications between an attorney and an expert witness . [Last updated in August 2023 by Jim Robinson, Esq., JurisPro Expert Witness Directory ] Keywords attorney-client privilege confidentiality Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS trial process/advocacy THE LEGAL PROCESS evidence legal practice/ethics wex definitions courts and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a restriction that prevents the receiving party from sharing appraisal information with their legal counsel without prior consent, which potentially interferes with the attorney-client relationship and the ability to receive informed legal advice. The Law Explanation states attorneys have a duty to provide competent representation, which requires access to all relevant facts. The changed contract text hinders this process. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines attorney-client privilege as protecting confidential communications between a lawyer and their client, which relates to the client's seeking legal advice or services. While the changed text doesn't explicitly violate attorney-client privilege (since the communication hasn't happened yet), it restricts the ability to even have that communication without prior consent, undermining the principle of seeking legal advice. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the potential infringement on the right to seek legal advice.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that due to the sensitive nature of the Confidential Informati on, any breach of this Agreement would cause irreparable ha rm to the Disclosing Party and that the Disclosing Party shall therefore be entitled to equitable relief in additi on to all other remedies available at law.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that due to the sensitive nature of the Confidential Information, any breach of this Agreement, regardless of materiality or actual harm, would cause irreparable harm to the Disclosing Party, and that the Disclosing Party shall therefore be entitled to equitable relief, including immediate injunctive relief, in addition to all other remedies available at law.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that 'any breach, regardless of materiality or actual harm, would cause irreparable harm' is likely unenforceable. Courts generally require a showing of actual irreparable harm to grant injunctive relief. This modification attempts to circumvent that requirement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 65 outlines the requirements for issuing preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders. A key requirement is demonstrating a likelihood of irreparable harm. The modified text attempts to bypass this requirement by stating that ANY breach causes irreparable harm, regardless of the circumstances. This contradicts the legal standard that requires a case-by-case assessment of actual harm.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the legal requirements for obtaining injunctive relief. The original text allows for equitable relief in case of a breach. The changed text asserts that 'any breach, regardless of materiality or actual harm, would cause irreparable harm,' and seeks 'immediate injunctive relief'. This contradicts Rule 65, which implies that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage must be shown to warrant a temporary restraining order. By claiming all breaches cause irreparable harm, the contract attempts to bypass the requirement of demonstrating actual irreparable harm on a case-by-case basis as outlined in Rule 65(b)(1)(A).

perturbation.original_text: Accordingly, Receiving Party acknowledges that a breach of this confidentiality agreement with respect to this spec ific Confidential Information which results in such info rmation becoming known to STAFF and/or Patients wil l entitle Disclosing Party to liquidated damages in t he sum of $250,000.00, it being expressly understoo d and agreed that such sum is a genuine pre-estimate of damages and not penalty.
perturbation.changed_text: Accordingly, Receiving Party acknowledges that any disclosure of Confidential Information, regardless of whether it results in damages to the Disclosing Party, will entitle Disclosing Party to liquidated damages in the sum of $250,000.00, it being expressly understood and agreed that such sum is a genuine pre-estimate of damages and not a penalty.
perturbation.explanation: Liquidated damages clauses must be a reasonable estimate of actual damages and not a penalty. Removing the requirement that the breach result in actual damages makes the $250,000 payment more likely to be viewed as an unenforceable penalty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 356 and general contract law principles state that liquidated damages must be a reasonable forecast of just compensation for the harm that is caused by the breach and that the harm is difficult or impossible to estimate accurately. If the sum is disproportionate to the likely actual damages caused by a breach, it will be deemed a penalty and is unenforceable.  Removing the 'results in such information becoming known to STAFF and/or Patients' clause makes the liquidated damage clause look more like a penalty than a reasonable estimation of damages.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: liquidated damages | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you liquidated damages Liquidated damages are an exact amount of money, or a set formula to calculate the amount of money, a party will owe if it breaches a contract , in order to compensate the injured party for its losses. Liquidated damages must be clearly stated in a section or clause of a contract and agreed upon by the parties prior to entering a contract. Liquidated damages are a variety of actual damages and a remedy for breach of contract. Parties to a contract use liquidated damages where actual damages, though real, are difficult or impossible to prove. Liquidated damages provide a clear monetary value to compensate the injured party while saving time and resources on litigation determining compensatory damages . Liquidated damages can also be used to deter parties from breaching contracts. Legal disputes surrounding liquidated damages arise following a breach of contract when either the injured party believes the liquidated damages are not adequately compensatory or the breaching party believes the liquidated damages are unreasonably high. Courts will not impose liquidated damages if the clause is punitive , illegal , unconscionable , or contrary to public policy . Example Undisclosed source code has value as a trade secret . Openly publishing the source code destroys the trade secret value. The trade secret value of source code is difficult or impossible to prove. There is no open market for "secret source code". In the 1997 equity case of Sun Microsystems, Inc. vs. Microsoft , the plaintiff Sun Microsystems made several damage claims. One was for $35 million in liquidated damages for Java source code disclosure, a contract violation. The case was later settled on terms, including a $20 million payment by Microsoft to Sun. [Last reviewed in June of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts legal practice/ethics business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the legal principle that liquidated damages must be a reasonable estimate of actual damages and not a penalty. The original text required the breach to result in information becoming known to staff/patients. The changed text allows for liquidated damages even without actual damages. Wex Law snippet indicates that courts will not impose liquidated damages if the clause is punitive. By removing the requirement for actual damages, the clause becomes more likely to be viewed as punitive and therefore unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” shall mean information that is used in Disclosing Party's business and is: (i) proprietary to, about or created by Disclosing Part y; (ii) gives Disclosing Party some competitive business ad vantage or the opportunity of obtaining such advantage or the disclosure of which could be detri mental to the interests of Disclosing Party; (iii) designated as Confidential Information by Disclosin g Party, or from all the relevant circumstances should reasonably be assumed by Receiving Party to be confidential and proprietary to Disclosing Party including information and documentation clear ly and conspicuously marked with a legend identifying its Confidential, Classified and Propri etary in nature: (iv) not generally known by Receiving Party
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” shall mean any information, regardless of its nature or source, that the Disclosing Party unilaterally designates as confidential, even if such information is publicly available or already known to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This modification broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include anything the Disclosing Party designates, even if it's already public. This is likely unenforceable because information already in the public domain cannot be protected as confidential. A standard NDA does not cover public information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (and similar state laws defining trade secrets and confidential information) requires that information be secret and have economic value from not being generally known.  The modified text attempts to protect information that is already publicly available, which directly contradicts the requirement of secrecy and is thus unenforceable. An NDA cannot protect information already in the public domain.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Confidential Information' to include publicly available information, which contradicts the fundamental requirement of secrecy inherent in trade secret and confidential information law, as explained in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation states that information already in the public domain cannot be protected as confidential. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), implying that trade secrets derive their value from not being generally known. By attempting to protect public information, the modified contract directly opposes this principle, creating a legal contradiction. The changed text seeks to protect information outside the scope of trade secret law.
